title: Stable magnetic nanodomains engineered via Ga+-ion irradiation for deterministic sequential switching
authors: Gijs W. A. Simons, Rik F. J. van Haren, Bert Koopmans
abstract: Precise control of magnetic domain formation at the nanoscale remains constrained by stochastic defect-mediated and unstable pinning, limiting scalability and reproducibility in spintronic architectures. Here we demonstrate that spatially engineered anisotropy gradients provide a deterministic alternative. Using focused Ga+-ion irradiation, we pattern magnetic energy landscapes containing nanoscale "anisotropy wells" that confine magnetic domain walls and enable bidirectional sequential switching without reliance on difficult-to-control material disorder. An analytical framework describing domain-wall energetics in graded anisotropy profiles yields predictive design rules for depinning and stability, which are supported by micromagnetic simulations and experiments. We realize programmable multi-domain configurations in continuous ferromagnetic films and demonstrate robust, reproducible switching of 750 nm regions, while first results for 100 nm are shown, approaching the theoretical limit set by the domain-wall width. By replacing unstable pinning with engineered energy landscapes, this anisotropy landscape establishes a scalable materials strategy for deterministic magnetic-state programming and opens a pathway toward dense, energy-efficient spintronic and reconfigurable magnetic nanodevices.
summary: This research presents a method to deterministically control magnetic domain switching in nanoscale spintronic devices by using focused ion irradiation to engineer precise, spatially varying magnetic anisotropy gradients. By replacing random pinning sites with predictable 'anisotropy wells,' the authors achieve robust, sequential, and programmable magnetic state switching, opening a scalable pathway for next-generation memory and logic devices.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving stable, deterministic, and scalable control over magnetic domain formation at the nanoscale, overcoming limitations imposed by stochastic defect-mediated pinning.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that engineering spatially varying magnetic anisotropy gradients using focused Ga+-ion irradiation provides a deterministic alternative to conventional pinning mechanisms, enabling robust, sequential, and programmable magnetic-state switching.
method: The work combines analytical modeling of domain-wall energetics with experimental realization using ion irradiation to pattern anisotropy wells in ferromagnetic thin films, validated by micromagnetic simulations and AHE/MOKE measurements.
model_or_system: The system involves perpendicularly magnetized Pt/Co/Pt multilayers where the effective magnetic anisotropy ($K_{	ext{eff}}$) is locally modified by ion irradiation. The theoretical framework models domain-wall (DW) energetics in graded anisotropy profiles, predicting depinning fields based on anisotropy contrast ($\Delta K$).
key_observables: Sequential magnetization plateaus observed in AHE hysteresis loops, the magnitude of the depinning field ($H_{	ext{pin}}$), and the dependence of $K_{	ext{eff}}$ on ion dose.
important_parameters: Anisotropy contrast ($\Delta K$), well width ($\delta$), domain-wall width ($\lambda$), and the characteristic length scale of the patterned regions (approaching 100 nm).
main_assumptions: The analytical model assumes a fixed DW profile and neglects thermal activation and microstructural disorder, providing upper-bound estimates for pinning fields.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematic anisotropy landscapes, calculated DW energy profiles, and experimental/simulated hysteresis loops showing distinct, sequential switching steps corresponding to engineered anisotropy wells.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the problem of stochastic pinning to developing an analytical model for anisotropy-controlled pinning, demonstrating this control via ion irradiation on patterned devices, and finally validating the scalability and determinism of the engineered energy landscape.
why_interesting: While focused on magnetism, the concept of engineering a potential energy landscape (the anisotropy profile) to guide the dynamics of a system (the domain wall) is analogous to designing engineered potentials in quantum systems to control quantum state evolution or tunneling pathways.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 a) Schematic illustration of a focused Ga+-ion beam locally modifying an initially perpendicularly magnetized Pt/Co/Pt stack. Ion irradiation reduces the perpendicular magnetic anisotropy Keff in a dose-dependent manner, enabling continuous and spatially resolved tailoring of the magnetic energy landscape. b) Schematic anisotropy landscape across the transition between two magnetic domains with distinct anisotropy levels within the 2 × 2 array, separated by an engineered low-anisotropy well. The resulting stepped hysteresis behavior reflects deterministic and sequential magnetization reversal of the individual domains. c) Corresponding magnetic response in the absence of anisotropy...

Fig. 2 a) Schematic anisotropy profile consisting of a well with width δ and depth Kwell relative to the surrounding material. b) Calculated energy of a Bloch-type domain wall EDW as a function of its position q under an external mag- netic field ⃗H. c) Derivative dEDW

Fig. 3 The calculated forward and backward depinning fields of a Bloch wall (Aex = 13 pJm−1) inside a single anisotropy well (Kwell = −0.2 MJ/m3, Keff = 0.5 MJ/m3) as a function of anisotropy contrast ∆K and well width δ. The field difference between the two directions increases with ∆K and saturates as the well becomes wider than the domain-wall width.

Fig. 4 An example of a four-region anisotropy profile comprising three anisotropy wells (Keff = 0.3 MJm−3, Kwell = −0.2 MJm−3, ∆K = 0.1 MJm−3, and δ = 15 nm). a) Corresponding energy landscape and b) depinning- field contrast between adjacent regions as a function of region width w. Each region supports a distinct minimum, enabling deterministic sequential switching. The contrast saturates once w ≳50 nm, defining the minimum size for stable, independently pinned regions.

Fig. 5 Micromagnetic validation of anisotropy- well–induced sequential switching. a) simulation of a 400 × 400 × 1 nm3 Co film patterned into six regions separated by anisotropy wells, using K0 = 0.1 MJm−3, Kwell = −0.2 MJm−3, ∆K = 0.03 MJm−3, δ = 15 nm, and w = 50 nm. Step-like switching in the hysteresis loop reflects sequential depinning of the domain wall. b) Simu- lation of a 340×340×1 nm3 film patterned into 25 regions arranged in a grid with anisotropy values increasing in a serpentine order from 0.10 to 0.82 MJm−3, starting from the top-left corner. The full hysteresis loop (orange) and field-pulse-induced switching sequence (purple) demon- strate the ability to program complex...
